does: This paper introduces a behavioral measurement framework for quantifying trust between AI agents based on costly verification in a cooperative survival game. The core contribution is operationalizing trust as the reduction in verification effort when an agent believes a teammate to be reliable, measured against a memoryless baseline. The authors test this framework across six frontier language model snapshots, finding that larger models (Claude Opus 4.6, Claude Sonnet 4.6, GPT-5.1, Gemini 3.1 Pro) reduce verification by 60-85% when paired with consistently reliable teammates, while smaller models show minimal adjustment. The framework captures not just trust formation but also breakage following failures and recovery dynamics, revealing that different models concentrate scrutiny differently—some target specific culprits while others become generally cautious—and that recovery operates slower than initial formation.
connects: This work builds on emerging research in multi-agent AI systems and agent reasoning under uncertainty. It extends prior work studying how language models make decisions under incomplete information and collaborate in team settings. The framework responds to the lack of standardized metrics for inter-agent trust, complementing prior behavioral economics approaches to trust measurement adapted for artificial systems. The survival game experimental paradigm draws on established game-theoretic designs for studying cooperation and information exchange, while the focus on costly verification reflects rational-choice principles from organizational behavior research. The study of asymmetric team composition and failure modes connects to broader inquiries into agent robustness, error handling, and team dynamics in complex multi-agent environments.
enables: This measurement framework enables systematic study of how AI teams should calibrate cooperation versus verification, with direct implications for governing multi-agent deployment. The behavioral metrics developed here could inform design of safety mechanisms where trust discounts are tuned to match actual agent reliability patterns. The finding that trust formation and recovery differ across models suggests model selection criteria for teams where interpersonal trust dynamics matter. Recovery slowness and failure-clustering effects point toward governance questions about how to structure multi-agent teams to prevent cascading distrust. Future work can use this framework to investigate optimal team compositions, design interventions to accelerate recovery from failures, and develop accountability mechanisms that align agent trust with actual performance. The approach also enables studying whether agents can be trained to develop more appropriate trust calibration.
